title: Examining Scaling and Transfer of Language Model Architectures for Machine Translation

structured.summary: This paper presents a comprehensive empirical study comparing language model (LM) architectures and encoder-decoder (EncDec) models for machine translation across bilingual, multilingual, and zero-shot settings. It finds that while architectural differences significantly impact small models, these gaps diminish at scale, with PrefixLM variants improving zero-shot translation quality by reducing off-target errors. The work offers insights into masking schemes, layer usage, and scaling behaviors, guiding future NMT architecture design.

strengths.point: Comprehensive empirical evaluation across diverse datasets and scaling regimes
strengths.evidence: Experiments span bilingual (WMT14 En-De/Fr, WMT19 En-Zh), multilingual (WMT many-to-many), and massively multilingual (OPUS-100) datasets, with systematic variation in model size and data conditions.

strengths.point: Identification of scaling behavior showing diminishing architectural gaps at large scale
strengths.evidence: Results demonstrate that performance differences between EncDec and LM variants narrow as model size increases, with deeper LMs outperforming wider ones.

strengths.point: Demonstration that full-visible masking and top-layer source encoding improve LM translation quality
strengths.evidence: PrefixLM with full-visible masking and TopOnly variant achieve translation quality competitive with EncDec models on supervised tasks.

strengths.point: Improved zero-shot translation quality and reduced off-target translation with PrefixLM
strengths.evidence: PrefixLM variants outperform EncDec on zero-shot directions and significantly reduce off-target translation errors.

strengths.point: Insightful analysis of masking schemes and source-side language modeling loss effects
strengths.evidence: Findings show causal masking and source-side language modeling loss harm translation quality, informing architectural design choices.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient comparison and positioning relative to prior LM-based NMT models
weaknesses.evidence: Lack of explicit discussion or experimental comparison to influential prior works like MASS, mBART, or GPT-based translation models weakens novelty claims.

weaknesses.point: Lack of theoretical or representational justification for full-visible masking benefits
weaknesses.evidence: No probing experiments or alignment analyses are provided to explain why full-visible masking improves translation quality.

weaknesses.point: Limited ablation and analysis of the TopOnly variant and layer usage
weaknesses.evidence: No exploration of intermediate or combined layer encodings to validate or optimize the top-only source encoding choice.

weaknesses.point: No exploration of hybrid architectures combining parameter sharing and separate encoding
weaknesses.evidence: Study contrasts fully shared LM variants with EncDec but omits hybrids that might balance representation disentanglement and parameter efficiency.

weaknesses.point: Unclear if training compute budgets and hyperparameter tuning are equivalent across models and scales
weaknesses.evidence: No detailed information on training steps, learning rates, or optimization protocols per architecture, limiting confidence in scaling conclusions.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation metrics for zero-shot translation limited to BLEU and off-target accuracy
weaknesses.evidence: Absence of human evaluation or semantic metrics (e.g., COMET, BLEURT) reduces confidence in zero-shot translation quality claims.

weaknesses.point: Missing critical training and dataset preprocessing details hindering reproducibility
weaknesses.evidence: No explicit description of tokenization, vocabulary sizes, optimizer settings, or training schedules.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient explanation and quantification of computational efficiency claims
weaknesses.evidence: Efficiency comparisons lack detailed metrics such as FLOPs, latency, or memory usage.

weaknesses.point: No analysis explaining lack of LM benefits for low-resource language transfer
weaknesses.evidence: No error analysis or hypotheses are provided to understand why LMs do not improve low-resource transfer.

weaknesses.point: Off-target translation remains a major issue without proposed mitigation strategies
weaknesses.evidence: While PrefixLM reduces off-target errors, no methods are proposed or evaluated to further address this challenge.

weaknesses.point: Limited generalizability discussion beyond machine translation
weaknesses.evidence: No evaluation or discussion of applicability to other sequence-to-sequence tasks.

weaknesses.point: Lack of analysis of performance variation across language families and typologies
weaknesses.evidence: No breakdown of multilingual results by language characteristics in OPUS-100.

weaknesses.point: No reporting on training stability, convergence speed, or hyperparameter sensitivity
weaknesses.evidence: These practical factors are critical but remain unexamined.

weaknesses.point: No consideration of pretrained vs training-from-scratch paradigms
weaknesses.evidence: Given prevalence of pretrained models, omission limits practical relevance.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient explanation and visualization of scaling exponents and irreducible loss
weaknesses.evidence: Limited clarity for readers unfamiliar with scaling laws.

questions.question: How do the proposed LM architectures compare empirically and conceptually to prior LM-based NMT models like MASS or mBART?
questions.motivation: Clarifying this would strengthen novelty claims and contextualize contributions within existing literature.

questions.question: What theoretical or representational mechanisms underlie the observed benefits of full-visible masking and top-layer source encoding?
questions.motivation: Understanding this could guide principled architectural design and generalization beyond empirical observations.

questions.question: Can hybrid architectures that partially share parameters between encoder and decoder offer better trade-offs in performance and efficiency?
questions.motivation: Exploring this could yield more practical and scalable NMT models.

questions.question: How sensitive are the results to training hyperparameters, compute budgets, and optimization strategies across architectures and scales?
questions.motivation: Ensuring fair comparison is critical for valid conclusions about scaling behavior.

questions.question: Would incorporating human evaluation or semantic similarity metrics alter conclusions about zero-shot translation quality?
questions.motivation: More robust evaluation could confirm or refine claims about zero-shot improvements.

questions.question: What strategies could further mitigate off-target translation in zero-shot settings beyond architectural changes?
questions.motivation: Addressing this major bottleneck is essential for practical zero-shot NMT deployment.

questions.question: How do vocabulary size and tokenization choices impact model capacity and transfer in massively multilingual settings?
questions.motivation: These factors critically affect multilingual model performance and reproducibility.

questions.question: Do the scaling behaviors and architectural insights generalize to other sequence-to-sequence tasks beyond translation?
questions.motivation: Broader applicability would increase the impact of the findings.

critique_points.point_001: Insufficient comparison and positioning relative to prior LM-based NMT models. Lack of explicit discussion or experimental comparison to influential prior works like MASS, mBART, or GPT-based translation models weakens novelty claims
critique_points.point_002: Lack of theoretical or representational justification for full-visible masking benefits. No probing experiments or alignment analyses are provided to explain why full-visible masking improves translation quality
critique_points.point_003: Limited ablation and analysis of the TopOnly variant and layer usage. No exploration of intermediate or combined layer encodings to validate or optimize the top-only source encoding choice
critique_points.point_004: No exploration of hybrid architectures combining parameter sharing and separate encoding. Study contrasts fully shared LM variants with EncDec but omits hybrids that might balance representation disentanglement and parameter efficiency
critique_points.point_005: Unclear if training compute budgets and hyperparameter tuning are equivalent across models and scales. No detailed information on training steps, learning rates, or optimization protocols per architecture, limiting confidence in scaling conclusions
critique_points.point_006: Evaluation metrics for zero-shot translation limited to BLEU and off-target accuracy. Absence of human evaluation or semantic metrics (e.g., COMET, BLEURT) reduces confidence in zero-shot translation quality claims
critique_points.point_007: Missing critical training and dataset preprocessing details hindering reproducibility. No explicit description of tokenization, vocabulary sizes, optimizer settings, or training schedules
critique_points.point_008: Insufficient explanation and quantification of computational efficiency claims. Efficiency comparisons lack detailed metrics such as FLOPs, latency, or memory usage
critique_points.point_009: No analysis explaining lack of LM benefits for low-resource language transfer. No error analysis or hypotheses are provided to understand why LMs do not improve low-resource transfer
critique_points.point_010: Off-target translation remains a major issue without proposed mitigation strategies. While PrefixLM reduces off-target errors, no methods are proposed or evaluated to further address this challenge
critique_points.point_011: Limited generalizability discussion beyond machine translation. No evaluation or discussion of applicability to other sequence-to-sequence tasks
critique_points.point_012: Lack of analysis of performance variation across language families and typologies. No breakdown of multilingual results by language characteristics in OPUS-100
critique_points.point_013: No reporting on training stability, convergence speed, or hyperparameter sensitivity. These practical factors are critical but remain unexamined
critique_points.point_014: No consideration of pretrained vs training-from-scratch paradigms. Given prevalence of pretrained models, omission limits practical relevance
critique_points.point_015: Insufficient explanation and visualization of scaling exponents and irreducible loss. Limited clarity for readers unfamiliar with scaling laws
